what_it_does: It is a design-note noticeboard entry arguing that two annotation modes are represented by one envelope schema, with only target and edit_target fields discriminated by mode, and that the content tree and UI tree are two families of a single typed-tree node-type registry.
observations: Frontmatter identifies it as a current block item in the dragnet-development channel, authored by ch-3mpkjg3r, linked as part_of board://item-e42d651f.
Title frames the note as P16 section 4 about the data-model truth of two modes and one schema.
Body claims the two modes differ only in target (board://node vs ui://element) and edit_target (board/file vs ui://→code://).
It asserts that all other envelope concerns—node-type, scale, quote-anchor, intent, attachments, frame, lifecycle, threading—are identical across modes.
It states the address grammar already supports about 19 schemes through one resolver, making the annotation layer target-scheme-agnostic.
It describes the node-type tree as shared: content tree and UI tree are two instances of one typed-tree pattern walked identically by the gesture.
standouts: The strongest architectural claim: one envelope type, N target schemes, one resolver.
Only two fields (target and edit_target) vary by mode; everything else is shared.
Content tree and UI tree are two families within one node-type registry.
conventions: Uses YAML frontmatter with id, address, type, source, state, title, author_session, channel, links, timestamps, and history.
Markdown body with a section heading and explanatory prose.
References address schemes (board://, ui://, code://, image://, decision://) inline.
As a noticeboard item, it is a prose/design substrate rather than executable code.
concerns: Created and updated timestamps are identical, so the history entry may not reflect any later edits.
Relies on external context (Tim's two modes, item-e42d651f) that is not defined in this file.
Claims ~19 address schemes without enumerating them or showing the resolver.
No code symbols or concrete schema definitions are present to verify the assertion.
notes: A design-note entry that makes a data-model case for unifying annotation envelopes and node-type trees across content and UI targets.
questions: What are Tim's two modes in concrete product terms?
What is the complete envelope schema and which fields are included?
What are the ~19 address schemes and how is the resolver implemented?
How does the node-type registry represent the two families and their node types?
What is the content of the parent item-e42d651f?
purpose_vs_actual: Matches: the title and body both present a data-model argument for a single annotation envelope schema shared across modes and target schemes.
intention_signals: It is trying to establish and record a design principle: future annotation targets and modes should reuse one envelope schema and one node-type registry rather than growing separate models.
novelty: It treats annotation targets as arbitrary address schemes resolved uniformly, and collapses content and UI hierarchies into one typed-tree pattern with two families.
contribution: A compact design rationale that a unified envelope schema and shared node-type registry can accommodate multiple target schemes and annotation modes without duplicating structure.
summary_for_embedding: This noticeboard item is a design note asserting that two annotation modes share a single envelope schema differentiated only by target and edit_target fields, and that content and UI trees are instances of a shared typed-tree pattern.
intention_for_embedding: It was filed to record and argue for a target-scheme-agnostic data model for annotations.
